text.title: Multi-Agent Design Patterns — Core Rules
text.summary: Five testable rules that gate any application of the multi-agent-design-patterns methodology. Sources: Anthropic — Building Effective Agents (engineering blog 2024); OpenAI Agents SDK documentation; LangGraph multi-agent patterns.

rule.statement: Pick the multi-agent pattern based on task decomposability: orchestrator-worker for parallelisable subtasks, hierarchical for tree-shaped projects, sequential for pipeline tasks, debate for consensus problems.
rule.rationale: Mis-picked patterns make the system harder to debug than a single-agent baseline; the decomposition shape is the load-bearing signal.

rule.statement: Use a model ladder: orchestrator = Claude Opus / GPT-4o, workers = Sonnet / GPT-4o-mini, classifiers = Haiku / GPT-3.5; never run the entire mesh on the most expensive model.
rule.rationale: Naive uniform-Opus deployments cost 5–20× more than tiered ones with no measurable quality lift.

rule.statement: Define an explicit handoff contract per agent edge: input schema, output schema, error-channel, max-turns; never implicit-pass-through arbitrary chat history.
rule.rationale: Implicit context-passing causes silent context drift and is the #1 cause of multi-agent quality regressions.

rule.statement: Wire a global termination condition (max steps OR cumulative cost OR done-tool fired) that overrides any individual agent's loop.
rule.rationale: Without a global cap, a single mis-routed handoff can run a workflow into the ground; emergency shutdown must be explicit, not emergent.

rule.statement: Emit a tracing span for every agent invocation and every cross-agent edge (input, output, latency, cost, model id); store traces for replay.
rule.rationale: Multi-agent regressions are unreproducible without traces; the replay capability is what distinguishes a system from a demo.
